mnemonics.title: STORY Framework
mnemonics.content:
S - Scenario/Setting (context)
T - Trigger (user action)
O - Outcome (system response)
R - Reaction (emotional state)
Y - Yield (next step)

Use: Remember the 5 elements to include in each storyboard frame.

mnemonics.title: FIVE STAR Storyboard
mnemonics.content:
F - Frame number and sequence
I - Interaction (what user does)
V - Visual representation
E - Emotional state indicator
S - System response
T - Touchpoint (where interaction occurs)
A - Annotation/notes
R - Result (expected outcome)

Use: Checklist for creating comprehensive storyboard panels.

mnemonics.title: Rapid Prototype REMEMBER
mnemonics.content:
R - Rough sketches
E - Early feedback
M - Modify easily
E - Explore alternatives
M - Measure with users
B - Bring clarity
E - Engage stakeholders
R - Refine continuously

Use: Remember benefits of low-fidelity paper prototyping in storyboarding.

acronyms.usage: The overall experience of a user when interacting with a product, encompassing all aspects of the user's journey.
The visual and interactive elements through which users interact with a product.
A design process sequence: Storyboard first for high-level flow, then task flows, then overview, then detailed wireframes.
Question to ask for each storyboard frame: what benefit/value does this screen provide to the user.
Design principles to apply when creating visual elements in higher-fidelity storyboards.

visualTips.visualization: Think of each frame like a comic strip panel - screen on left (40%), action text on right (30%), annotations at bottom (30%). Use consistent borders and numbered sequences.
Draw arrows from bottom-right of one frame to top-left of next frame. Use different arrow styles: solid for main path, dashed for alternative paths, red for error states.
Use simple icons above each frame: 🙂 for positive, 😐 for neutral, 😟 for frustration, 😲 for surprise. Helps identify pain points quickly.
Show device silhouette (phone/desktop), environment (home/office/outdoor), and time indicator around frame corners to establish context.
Place numbered circles (1,2,3...) on the frame and corresponding notes in a legend box at the bottom of the storyboard sheet.

analogies.analogy: Just like a movie director uses storyboards to plan scenes before filming, UX designers use storyboards to 'direct' the user's journey before building the product.
A storyboard is like a GPS route - it shows the path from start (user goal) to destination (task completion), highlighting stops and turns along the way.
Each storyboard frame is like a panel in a comic strip - it captures a moment in the narrative with visual and text elements that together tell the full story.
Low-fidelity storyboards are to designers what rough sketches are to architects - quick, easy to change, focus on structure not decoration.
